diagrams.title: Python Function Structure
diagrams.description: A block diagram showing the anatomy of a Python function with all its components
elements: Function Header: 'def' keyword, function name, parameter list in parentheses
Parameter Box: Contains positional, keyword, default, *args, **kwargs parameters
Function Body: Indented block with docstring, statements, and logic
Return Statement: Returns value(s) to caller using 'return' keyword
Arrow showing flow from parameters → body → return value
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the complete structure of Python functions including syntax, parameters, and return values essential for IoT programming

diagrams.title: Function Call Execution Flow
diagrams.description: A flowchart showing step-by-step execution when a function is called in an IoT context
elements: Start Node: Function call with arguments
Decision Diamond: Check if arguments match parameters
Path A (Match): Arguments assigned to parameters, function body executes
Path B (Mismatch): TypeError or missing argument exception
Processing Node: Execute function body statements
Return Node: Return value sent back to caller
End Node: Continue program execution after function returns
IoT Example Box: Sensor reading function → process data → return temperature
diagrams.purpose: Visualizes the complete lifecycle of function execution helping students understand how data flows in IoT sensor processing functions

diagrams.title: Parameter Types Classification
diagrams.description: A classification diagram showing all five types of function parameters in Python
elements: Positional Parameters: Order matters, assigned first
Keyword Parameters: Name=value syntax, order independent
Default Parameters: Have predefined values, used when argument omitted
*args (Variable Positional): Accepts tuple of extra positional arguments
**kwargs (Variable Keyword): Accepts dictionary of extra keyword arguments
Icons showing each parameter type with syntax examples
Arrows showing precedence order in function definition
diagrams.purpose: Students understand when and how to use each parameter type for flexible IoT function design

diagrams.title: Variable Scope in Functions
diagrams.description: Three concentric circles showing Local, Enclosing, and Global scope boundaries
elements: Innermost Circle: Local scope - variables defined inside function
Middle Circle: Enclosing scope - variables in outer (enclosing) function
Outermost Circle: Global scope - variables defined at module level
Arrows showing 'global' and 'nonlocal' keyword usage
Example variables: sensor_data (local), device_id (global), calibration_factor (enclosing)
LEGB Rule label: Local → Enclosing → Global → Built-in
diagrams.purpose: Helps students understand variable visibility to prevent bugs in IoT applications where global device configurations need to be accessed

diagrams.title: Lambda Function Structure
diagrams.description: Comparison diagram showing regular function vs lambda (anonymous) function
elements: Regular Function Block: def keyword, name, parameters, docstring, body, return
Lambda Function Block: lambda keyword, parameters, single expression, implicit return
Side-by-side syntax comparison: 'def get_celsius(f): return (f-32)*5/9' vs 'lambda f: (f-32)*5/9'
Use Case Icons: Suitable for sorting, mapping, filtering in data processing
IoT Application: Lambda with map() for processing sensor array readings
diagrams.purpose: Students learn when to use lambda functions for concise code in IoT data processing pipelines

charts.title: Parameter Types Comparison Table
charts.content: Rows: Parameter Type | Syntax | When to Use | Example in IoT | Columns: Positional, Keyword, Default, *args, **kwargs; Shows syntax, use cases, and IoT-specific examples for each parameter type

charts.title: Function Design Decision Flowchart
charts.content: Start → Do you need to reuse code? → Yes → Define function → Does it need parameters? → Yes → Choose type (positional/keyword/default/*args/**kwargs) → Does it return value? → Yes → Add return statement → End; Each decision point shows IoT context (e.g., sensor reading, device config, data collection)

charts.title: Return Value Types in Python Functions
charts.content: Table showing: Single Value (returns one object), Multiple Values (returns tuple), Nothing (returns None), Custom Objects (returns class instances), IoT Examples for each: threshold_check returns boolean, read_sensors returns dict, process_data returns list of calibrated readings

charts.title: Function Classification for IoT Development
charts.content: Built-in Functions: print(), len(), range(), type() | User-Defined Functions: Custom sensor drivers, data processing | Lambda Functions: Quick data transformations | Recursive Functions: Tree traversal, hierarchical device networks | Generator Functions: Streaming sensor data | Each category with IoT-specific examples

branches: Definition & Syntax: def keyword, parameters, return, docstring
Variable Scope: local, global, nonlocal, LEGB rule
Return Values: single, multiple, None, objects
Special Types: lambda, recursive, generator functions
IoT Applications: sensor_read(), calibrate_sensor(), check_threshold(), send_alert()
Best Practices: single responsibility, meaningful names, docstrings, DRY principle
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting all function concepts to IoT practical applications showing how modular functions enable maintainable IoT code
